claim: WHO's 2024 rabies fact sheet estimates about 59,000 human rabies deaths annually, identifies dog bites and scratches as causing 99% of human rabies cases, and states that PEP includes wound washing, vaccine, and RIG when indicated.
method: Reviewed the WHO fact sheet key facts, overview, prevention, and PEP sections to anchor burden, transmission source, and the full PEP component chain.
limitations: Global fact sheet, not a country or district access dataset.
The 59,000-death figure is an estimate and WHO notes underreporting.
This source cannot identify which facilities stock vaccine, RIG, or monoclonal antibodies.

claim: The June 2024 Gavi, WHO, and United Against Rabies announcement states that all Gavi-eligible countries can apply for human rabies vaccine support for PEP, while RIG and dog vaccines are not covered by the programme.

source.title: Gavi to boost access to life-saving human rabies vaccines in over 50 countries: Gavi, WHO and UAR

method: Reviewed the WHO-hosted joint news release for eligibility, funding scope, excluded products, and application timing relevant to allocation decisions.
limitations: Announcement-level source; it does not provide country application contents, approved volumes, or delivery performance.
It cannot prove that funded vaccines reach rural bite victims or that RIG is available where indicated.
The source describes programme design, not independent implementation outcomes.

claim: WHO's 2018 rabies vaccine position paper provides global policy guidance on post-exposure and pre-exposure vaccination strategies, including recommendations on rabies immunoglobulin use in the PEP context.

source.title: Rabies vaccines: WHO position paper - April 2018

method: Reviewed the WHO publication page for document scope, date, SAGE review process, and stated focus on programmatic feasibility, simplified schedules, and cost-effectiveness.
limitations: Policy guidance, not a live implementation dataset.
Does not provide current country-level PEP access or stockout measures.
Clinical details require domain review before any operational use.

claim: A 2017-2018 assessment of 23 responding countries in Asia and Africa found major variation in public-sector rabies vaccine cost, availability, intradermal use, distribution systems, and reporting cadence; RIG was limited or unavailable in many countries and the assessment focused on vaccine.

source.title: Overview of rabies post-exposure prophylaxis access, procurement and distribution in selected countries in Asia and Africa, 2017-2018

method: Reviewed the CDC Stacks abstract, methods, results, and citation metadata to classify what the assessment can prove about PEP logistics and what it cannot prove about current facility access.
limitations: Convenience sample and key-informant assessment; not a nationally representative facility survey.
Data were collected from January 2017 to May 2018 and may not reflect current post-Gavi-support access.
Because RIG was limited or unavailable in many countries, the study focused on vaccine and cannot estimate complete PEP access.

claim: The WHO Rabies Modelling Consortium estimated that improved provision of rabies PEP in Gavi-eligible countries could avert additional deaths and remain cost-effective, while emphasizing that patient risk assessment matters for reducing unnecessary PEP use.

source.title: The potential effect of improved provision of rabies post-exposure prophylaxis in Gavi-eligible countries: a modelling study

method: Reviewed the stable DOI metadata and source summaries to anchor the existence of a PEP investment model and its relevance to Gavi-eligible countries.
limitations: Modelled estimates are not implementation proof and depend on assumptions about exposure, access, and programme uptake.
The model cannot identify current sub-national stockouts, completion, or RIG access.
The source supports tractability framing but not a country ranking without replication.

claim: WHO's Global Health Observatory rabies topic page provides a country-level reported human rabies deaths indicator and explicitly warns that rabies deaths, vaccine access, and animal-population occurrence data are limited by under-reporting, misdiagnosis, and under-diagnosis.
method: Reviewed the WHO GHO rabies topic page for available indicators and WHO's stated caveat about what reported rabies data can and cannot prove. Classified it as a reporting-cadence and burden-presence source, not a complete PEP access dataset.
limitations: The indicator is reported human rabies deaths, not bite incidence, PEP demand, vaccine stock, RIG access, or completion.
WHO explicitly warns that rabies is under-reported and mis- or under-diagnosed, so low reported deaths cannot be treated as low exposure or sufficient PEP access.
The page is country-level and does not provide facility-tier or district access measures.

claim: WHO's 2026 Weekly Epidemiological Record article on rabies prevention and control in the African Region states that robust human and animal surveillance, PEP risk-assessment decision trees, case investigation forms, and integrated bite case management are required to monitor progress toward ending dog-mediated human rabies deaths.

source.title: Rabies prevention and control in the WHO African Region

method: Reviewed the WHO publication page and WER summary for Africa-specific surveillance, PEP risk-assessment, IBCM, Gavi support, and One Health programme requirements. Classified it as a current programme-framing source rather than a country access dataset.
limitations: Regional WHO article; it does not publish a facility-level inventory of vaccine, RIG, monoclonal antibody, or PEP completion availability.
It names tools and programme capacities but does not prove that countries are using them consistently.
It cannot distinguish procurement bottlenecks from triage, reporting, or dog-vaccination bottlenecks without country records.

claim: WHO's rabies vaccination and immunization page classifies exposure categories and states that category III exposure requires immediate vaccination and rabies immunoglobulin, while intradermal vaccination is a safe, immunogenic, dose- and cost-sparing alternative to intramuscular vaccination.

source.title: Control of Neglected Tropical Diseases: Rabies vaccines

method: Reviewed WHO's rabies vaccination and immunization page for the category I, II, and III exposure rules, RIG indication, and intradermal regimen statement. Used it to anchor the bite-risk and regimen-capacity components of the inventory.
limitations: Guidance page, not a country implementation dataset.
It cannot prove that facilities can classify exposures correctly or administer RIG where category III exposure is indicated.
Operational use of clinical guidance requires domain review; this record is only for source classification.

claim: WOAH's WAHIS portal is the official animal-health reporting surface for WOAH-listed diseases and provides public access to domestic-animal, wildlife, emerging-disease, zoonosis, and historical animal-health reports relevant to dog-rabies context.
method: Reviewed the WOAH WAHIS description for reporting mandate, public interface, early-warning, monitoring, annual reporting, and archive scope. Classified it as animal-rabies and dog-vaccination context that must be paired with human PEP data.
limitations: WAHIS is an animal-health reporting system; it does not measure human bite victims, PEP access, RIG availability, or PEP completion.
Member reporting can vary in completeness, timeliness, and sub-national detail.
Animal rabies reports are context for human PEP demand, not proof of the binding human-health access bottleneck.

claim: CDC's 2026 rabies status-by-country page evaluates country rabies risk and access to high-quality PEP for US travel and animal-importation guidance, but it is not designed as a public allocation dataset for endemic-country PEP component gaps.
method: Reviewed the CDC page's key points, stated use cases, and definitions of rabies status and surveillance robustness. Classified it as rejected for canonical PEP access ranking but useful as an external risk-classification comparator.
limitations: The page is built for US traveler and importation recommendations, not for allocating endemic-country rabies programme resources.
It does not expose the component-level evidence needed to separate vaccine access, RIG access, bite-risk triage, reporting cadence, and dog-vaccination context.
A country risk category cannot substitute for source-level public records when screening PEP access bottlenecks.
